title: TPU v1 · Signal-Integrity Signoff: Crosstalk on Dense Array Wiring and Long Buses
oneLine: In TPU's 256×256 array and long Unified Buffer buses, coupling capacitance lets neighboring nets interfere. Aggressor switching changes victim delay—faster or slower—and may induce glitches. Crosstalk-aware STA such as PrimeTime SI folds delay shifts into setup/hold and analyzes noise on static nets, proving 700 MHz timing and functional noise immunity with coupled post-route parasitics.

legend.name: Aggressor
legend.desc: A switching net that injects coupled current into a neighbor. Synchronously toggling data/weight lines in the systolic array and wide, long buses to the 24 MiB Unified Buffer can form worst-case multi-aggressor groups. Any net may be aggressor in one relation and victim in another.

legend.name: Victim
legend.desc: The net whose delay or voltage is disturbed. A timing-path victim can lose setup/hold margin; a nominally static enable or control victim can receive a glitch. SI tools identify every victim and its coupled aggressors.

legend.name: Coupling capacitance Cc / mechanism
legend.desc: Sidewall capacitance between parallel metal lines couples aggressor transitions into victims. Same-direction switching can speed the victim and hurt hold; opposite-direction switching can slow it and hurt setup. Narrower spacing and longer parallel runs increase Cc, extracted in coupled SPEF.

legend.name: Two effects: delay Δd and noise glitch
legend.desc: For switching victims, SI converts coupling into positive or negative delay change under worst alignment and feeds it into STA. For static victims, it analyzes glitch height/width and propagation to prevent false capture. Both timing and functional noise must pass.

legend.name: Crosstalk-aware STA + SI gate
legend.desc: PrimeTime SI-class tools analyze setup/hold with coupled delay and static-noise glitches across MMMC. Illustratively, every path retains slack≥0 and every static-net glitch stays below receiver thresholds. Any failure returns to ⑧ physical ECO.

steps.cap: ① Extract coupled SPEF and enumerate each victim's aggressors and worst feasible switching combinations on dense array wiring and long UB buses.
② Convert coupling into Δd: same-direction switching speeds the victim and threatens hold; opposite-direction switching slows it and threatens setup.
③ Analyze noise on static victims, determining whether a coupled pulse can cross a receiver threshold, propagate, or be captured.
④ Run crosstalk-aware STA across MMMC, folding Δd into setup/hold and evaluating static noise to confirm 700 MHz robustness.
⑤ Pass only when crosstalk-aware slack≥0 and glitches remain below noise limits; otherwise repair routing in ⑧ and re-sign.

before: Use coupled post-route SPEF: SI requires extracted line-to-line coupling Cc; single-net RC cannot model crosstalk. Verify extraction fidelity in the array and long UB buses.
Define MMMC corners: Crosstalk is PVT-dependent. Analyze setup at slow corners with worst slowdown and hold at fast corners with worst speedup, across every required mode.

Define aggressor alignment and concurrency: Specify which aggressors can switch within a victim window, multi-aggressor superposition, probability, and timing windows. Loose assumptions overreport; tight assumptions miss real events.

Define delay and noise criteria: Require setup/hold slack≥0 after crosstalk and glitch height/width below receiver noise limits with no downstream propagation. Thresholds come from libraries/IP; values here are illustrative.
Define waiver rules: Allow only proven infeasible alignments or safely clamped victims, with documented evidence and approval, so low-risk noise does not hide true failures.
during: Sign both delay and noise: Crosstalk changes timing and can create functional glitches. Timing-only analysis can miss a static control net being falsely asserted, so both branches are mandatory.
Apply worst directions to the correct corners: Slow corner plus opposite-direction slowdown for setup; fast corner plus same-direction speedup for hold. Do not combine both with one generic corner.

Prioritize dense array wiring and long UB buses: Parallel synchronized lines in the 256×256 array produce multi-aggressor superposition, while long 24 MiB UB buses accumulate coupling. They are the primary victim sets at 700 MHz.

Separate real violations from pessimistic alignment: Correlate windows, logic dependence, and aggressor reachability to remove physically impossible worst-case reports without masking feasible crosstalk.

Converge noise-on-delay interactions: Crosstalk changes victim arrival, which changes aggressor alignment in the next iteration. Confirm the SI engine reaches a stable solution rather than oscillating.

Disclosure boundary: Crosstalk-aware STA, PrimeTime SI, coupled SPEF, MMMC, and aggressor/victim analysis are standard signoff methods. TPU's SI corners and reports are not public; all thresholds are illustrative.

result.label: Crosstalk setup (example)
result.value: Slow corner + opposite-direction slowdown; critical-path setup slack ≥0 including Δd

result.label: Crosstalk hold (example)
result.value: Fast corner + same-direction speedup; hold slack ≥0 on minimum paths

result.label: Static noise / glitch (example)
result.value: Every static victim glitch stays below receiver threshold and is neither propagated nor captured

result.label: Priority victim regions
result.value: Parallel array nets + long 24 MiB UB buses; checked for multi-aggressor and long-run coupling

result.label: Analysis mode
result.value: PrimeTime SI-class crosstalk-aware STA; MMMC; coupled SPEF; converged noise-on-delay

result.label: SI verdict (example)
result.value: All-corner slack≥0 and no functional glitch → 700 MHz preserved → PASS

after: Return violations to ⑧ for routing ECO: Increase spacing, insert grounded shields, change layer/direction, reduce aggressor drive, or buffer. Re-extract coupled SPEF and rerun SI after layout changes.
Avoid fixing one net by breaking another: A modified victim can become a new aggressor or alter nearby timing. Incrementally recheck all affected nets and corners.
Feed SI into the multidomain signoff gate: Crosstalk delay/noise joins base STA, power/EM/IR, DRC/LVS, and LEC. SI must be green before tapeout.
Archive traceable evidence: Preserve parasitic revision, corner×mode matrix, alignment settings, violations, waivers, and post-fix results. Every waiver needs rationale and ownership.
Tape out only when every dimension is green: Resolve or formally waive every SI issue and confirm all other signoff dimensions before release.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (the 256×256 array, 24 MiB Unified Buffer, 700 MHz, 28 nm, die≤331 mm², and weight-stationary design are published facts. Crosstalk-aware STA/PrimeTime SI, coupled SPEF, MMMC, aggressor/victim delay Δd, and static-noise analysis are standard signoff practice; all slack/noise thresholds are illustrative, not private TPU reports or corners.)
